figures.caption: Fig. 8: Sim-to-sim transfer in AirSim: a multi-drone swarm navigates a dense bamboo forest with photorealistic depth and stochastic wind. A policy trained in a lightweight differentiable simulator with spherical obstacles transfers to AirSim without retraining, showing robustness to visual domain shift and aerodynamic perturbations.

Fig. 7: Circle-swap with N = 64. Left: Panoramic (93.8% success, 4 collisions); Right: Forward-only (59.4%, 26 collisions). The swarm selforganizes a counterclockwise rotation through the congested center. Bottom: per-drone depth tiles; red X = frozen after collision.

Fig. 2: PanoDP training pipeline. At each time step the four onboard depth images are stitched into a 360◦ panorama, encoded by the circular CNN, fused with the body-frame state vector in a GRU cell, and mapped to an acceleration command. Because the dynamics integrator (13)–(14) is fully differentiable, the trajectory loss L can be back-propagated through the entire T -step unrolled graph (dashed arrows), yielding exact gradients ∂L/∂θ that update all learnable parameters in a single pass.

Fig. 4: Ablation training curves over 50 K iterations (6 metrics), with tail zoom (40–50 K). Full PanoDP converges most stably with lowest variance. Removing the GRU raises jerk and snap; removing circular convolutions or using a flat MLP yields higher plateaus.

Fig. 5: Ablation bar chart (tail-mean at 50 K iterations) across eight training metrics. PanoDP (Ours, red border) achieves the best or near-best value on every metric. Stars (⋆) mark the best method per subplot.

Fig. 3: Panorama and forward-depth training comparison over 50 K iterations (6 metrics). Insets zoom into the converged tail (40–50 K steps). PanoDP (blue, solid) consistently outperforms the forward-only baseline DPD† (grey, dashed) across all metrics.
